mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 3 Series 320i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 320i engine typically costs £900–£7,500 supply only depending on generation. M10/M20-era engines sit at the lower end (£900–£2,500), while B48 units run £3,200–£7,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 3 Series 320i engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends entirely on which of the ten engine generations your 320i has. The six-cylinder cars (E21–E46) are generally very reliable, with the M20 being one of BMW's most durable engines. The N43 (2007–2011) is widely considered one of the worst BMW engines ever made. The N20 has timing chain guide issues. The B48 (2015+) is a significant improvement. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 3 Series 320i?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain failure on the N43 and N20, cooling system failures on E36/E46 six-cylinder cars, valve cover gasket leaks on the M54 and N46, and NOx sensor issues on the N43. The N43 is the most problematic — timing chain guides break up and debris blocks the oil pickup.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 3 Series 320i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and generation. Against a well-kept £7,000 F30 320i, a £4,000 recon is 57% of the car's value — borderline. Against a tired £3,500 E90 example, the same recon exceeds our 70% BMW threshold — get a scrap valuation first. For B48-engined cars (post-2015), higher vehicle values make replacement more viable.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 3 Series 320i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: E21 (1975–1982) is M10B20, E30 (1982–1991) is M20B20, E36 (1991–1998) is M50B20/M52B20, E46 (1998–2005) is M52TUB20/M54B22, E90 (2005–2011) is N46B20/N43B20, F30 (2012–2019) is N20B20/B48B20, G20 (2019–present) is B48B20. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the N43 timing chain, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A preventative chain replacement (£800–£2,500) is far cheaper than a catastrophic chain snap requiring a full engine replacement. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately. Many specialists recommend preventative replacement at 60,000 miles regardless of symptoms on the N43.

eraMap.title: The 320i THROUGH TIME — Era Map

rows.eraNote: Original fuel-injected 320i — robust but now rare and ageing
Silky-smooth six-cylinder — one of BMW's most durable engines
Twin-cam six — reliable but cooling system requires attention
2.2-litre six — 170 bhp. Valve cover gasket and cooling system weak points
Last of the naturally aspirated fours — 150 bhp. Timing chain issues
Direct-injection four — timing chain and NOx sensor problems
First turbocharged 320i — 184 bhp. Timing chain guide failure risk
Modular turbo four — significantly improved reliability
Modern B48 — 184 bhp, 300 Nm. Coolant leaks and PCV wear

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page matches — Sections 2, 4 and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

rows.bestFor: Insurance jobs, warranty claims (modern cars only)

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current B48 generation, the most common quote request today. Older generations: see the Era Map above for M10, M20, M50, M52, M54, N46, and N43-specific pricing, which runs significantly lower.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a 3 Series 320i engine typically takes 8–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). Four-cylinder engines are generally more accessible; six-cylinder E30–E46 cars require more time.

cta.label: → Compare 3 Series 320i engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: N43 Timing Chain Failure (E90 2007–2011)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise from the engine on cold start, metallic ticking, engine warning light, loss of power. Chain guides can break up and block the oil pickup.
problems.typicalMileage: 30,000–100,000 miles — failures have been reported from just 30,000 miles.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only if caught early; catastrophic failure requires full engine replacement.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today.

problems.recommendation: The N43 is widely considered one of the worst engines BMW ever made. The excessive 18,000-mile service interval is a major contributor to failure — chain guides break up and debris blocks the oil pickup. A preventative chain replacement is essential on any high-mileage N43. At the first sign of cold-start rattle — or even without, as some fail without warning — book an inspection immediately.

problems.title: N20 Timing Chain Guide Failure (F30 2012–2015)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start, metallic ticking, engine warning light, loss of power.
problems.typicalMileage: 40,000–80,000 miles — early production cars (pre-March 2015) are most at risk.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only if caught early; catastrophic failure requires full engine replacement.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: at first sign of rattle, book inspection today.

problems.recommendation: The N20's timing chain tensioner can fail, causing the chain to slip or break. If the tensioner fails completely, it can lead to catastrophic engine failure. Early production N20s (pre-March 2015) are most susceptible. Many specialists recommend preventative chain replacement at 60,000–80,000 miles regardless of symptoms.

problems.title: Cooling System Failures (E36–E46 M50/M52/M54)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, overheating, steam from engine bay, low coolant warning.
problems.typicalMileage: 60,000–120,000 miles — age-related as plastic components become brittle.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; ignoring coolant warnings can lead to head gasket failure.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if the coolant warning comes on, pull over and let the engine cool.

problems.recommendation: The cooling systems on E36 and E46 six-cylinder 320is are well-documented weak points. The plastic expansion tank, water pump impeller, and thermostat housing all degrade with age. If you see any coolant warnings, have the system inspected immediately — overheating can cause head gasket failure on these otherwise robust engines.

rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — catches fault before catastrophic failure
Preventative — many specialists recommend at 60k miles
Simple fix — do it immediately to avoid head gasket failure
Common maintenance item — repair when symptoms appear
Interference engine — piston-valve contact means little is left to repair

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 320i? Check These First
buyIf: * Pre-2005 six-cylinder (E21–E46) with full service history — robust and characterful
* Post-2015 B48 (F30 LCI or G20) — significantly improved reliability over N20
* N20 with documented timing chain replacement (especially if pre-March 2015)
* Cooling system recently serviced on E36/E46 cars
avoidIf: * 2007–2011 N43 with cold-start rattle and no chain history — the worst 320i engine
* Pre-March 2015 N20 without timing chain documentation
* Overheating or coolant loss on any generation — can indicate head gasket failure
* Poor service history on any generation — these engines are unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 320i badge spans five decades and a huge range of engines — from the bulletproof M20 six to the problematic N43 four. The six-cylinder cars (E21–E46) are the most durable; the N43 is best avoided entirely; the N20 needs timing chain vigilance; and the B48 (2015+) is the modern sweet spot. With the 70% BMW scrap threshold, a £4,000 recon on a £6,000 320i is 67% — borderline. On a sub-£5,000 example, think carefully.

cta.label: → Compare 3 Series 320i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 3 Series 320i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling on startup (N43/N20 timing chain warning)
* Service History — Including which engine era and confirmed oil-interval discipline
* Coolant Check — Check level and condition — overheating kills these engines
* Timing Chain Documentation — Any evidence of chain replacement on N43/N20?
* Smoke Check — Blue smoke could indicate valve stem seals or piston rings
* Oil Leaks — Check around valve cover (M54/N46 common) and oil filter housing
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
